text: Hi, I'm Adrian and I'm here with Charles from Accenture. Today we're talking about
knowledge management in the oil and gas industry. Charles, can you tell me what
problems Accenture has solved for customers in the oil and gas industry?
So our clients tell us they are missing opportunity to buy raw oil due to the
lack of accurate or timely information and that costs them millions of dollars a year.
That sounds like an expensive problem. How does technology help here?
So the objective is to ingest documents coming from multiple sources in the client side. It could
be report of extraction, it could be process reporting, it could be many different things,
even internal communications. So we are extracting from the existing system of the client those
information and ingesting it to our system. So after that we will need to create a sort of
representation of our knowledge to index documents and after that the third step will be to retrieve
the information. So it's a three-step process. It is an ingestion process, it is a knowledge
representation process and it is a retrieval process. Both the ingestion and the retrieval have
common solution. So we have been putting a lot of time and effort on specifically on the knowledge
representation aspect. Okay, so how do the services that are here on the board help you with that?
So again, at the beginning we have documents coming in in multiple formats to our system. So they go
through our Lambda. The Lambda will work as a sort of orchestrator between the different services and create
also some logic on top of it. So first we have been training ahead of having the model the system
operational. We have been training our SageMaker instances to do some tasks that are really specific
to the oil and gas. We have been training models specifically for our client problems. So it could
be some document classification, it could be some key facts extractions, it could be multiple things. So we
have been training the model first and then after the Lambda will be consuming. So one information is coming to the
system. The Lambda will send the information, the raw data to the SageMaker to the model that has been trained in order to extract again some information.
Type of information, key facts of the document, things like that. Then the Lambda receives the
information from the SageMaker and then will call the Comprehend as a component. The Comprehend is really the
natural language processing component of AWS. This one goes at more the sentence level to try to analyze
the structure of a sentence. So for example, it does some name entity recognition or part of speech tagging,
things like that. And again, it's important because once I've extracted the facts, I want to understand
the structure of my facts. From there, I can start saving my data into my knowledge graph for future
retrieval and for creating my representation. Okay. What's the reason that you use a knowledge
graph here with Amazon Neptune? We use a knowledge graph for a few reasons, but the first one is
obviously it's a database. So it helps us to store and to persist data. But on top of that, a fundamental
difference between a knowledge graph and a traditional database, really SQL database, is that
it enables us to have a much more dynamic and flexible structure where SQL structure have to be
predetermined at the beginning of the project. So it evolves through times with new information coming
in. And that's where that's a fundamental aspect of why we picked the database, knowledge graph database.
Okay, so this flexible and evolving structure, can you perhaps illustrate that with an example?
Yes, so we call the different type of oil crude. So I ingested the first document and the document is about a crude type A.
Crude A, from the document I'm able to identify that it has a density and a sulfur level.
Then, at a later stage, I ingested another document regarding a crude B. This crude B has more information.
It has a density, it has a sulfur level, but this time it has another, it has a risk associated with it. So I can add to that relationship to my object.
That's one very important fact. On top of that, I'm able to calculate to compute on top of my data some interesting facts.
In this context, I'm able to see that well, if my crude B has a very similar density and sulfur than my crude A,
but the crude B also has this, which is a risk associated with the processing of the crude,
it means that there is a very high chance, there's a probability that there is also, that risk is also associated with my crude A.
So that's one of those elements, the dynamism and the flexibility of those database plus the computation part.
That sounds really powerful. Now, I noticed that you also have elastic search here on the board.
If I have this powerful knowledge graph, what's the reason that I still need elastic search?
So, again, the lambda, at the same time it will trigger this first process,
will also save, will also send the raw information to the elastic search to be indexed.
Why? Because the knowledge graph represents a series or a set of concepts that are related with each other,
but potentially we're going to, we would be missing information if we're not having another way to treat the information.
So here it's structured data, so we go from unstructured to structure,
but sometimes there is some pieces of information that are still very important, that are not meeting this structure.
So in this context, elastic search makes sure that we are not missing this information.
So if the user type a free text query, we are able to address, even if it's not in our Neptune knowledge graph,
we are still able to address it through the elastic search and to retrieve the information.
That's really interesting. Thank you for sharing this architecture.
And thank you for watching This is My Architecture. See you next episode.
